verses.flow: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus according to the command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, Strong's G652) denotes one sent with delegated authority, not merely a messenger. "Kat' epitagēn" (κατ' ἐπιταγήν) means "according to command"—Paul's apostleship rests on divine mandate, not self-appointment. THEOLOGICAL: God is here called "Savior" (sōtēr, σωτήρ), a title shared between the Father and Christ Jesus in the Pastoral Epistles, underscoring the unity of the divine saving purpose (cf. Titus 2:13).

verses.flow: To Timothy, a true child in faith: grace, mercy, peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Even as I urged you to remain in Ephesus while I was going into Macedonia, that you might charge certain ones not to teach other doctrine,
nor to give heed to myths and endless genealogies, which produce speculations rather than the stewardship of God that is in faith—
verses.note: GREEK: "Genealogíais aperántois" (γενεαλογίαις ἀπεράντοις)—"endless genealogies"—likely refers to speculative expansions of OT genealogies in Second Temple Jewish tradition or early proto-gnostic aeon lists. "Oikonomían theoú" (οἰκονομίαν θεοῦ) means "the stewardship/administration of God," pointing to God's ordered plan of salvation rather than human speculation. HISTORICAL: Ephesus was a crossroads of Jewish and Hellenistic speculation; the false teachers likely blended Torah interpretation with mythological elaboration.

verses.flow: But the end of the charge is love out of a pure heart and a good conscience and an unhypocritical faith,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "télos" (τέλος) of the charge is not merely its cessation but its purpose and goal—love (agápē). This love has a threefold source: a pure heart, a good conscience, and unhypocritical faith (písteos anypokrítou). GREEK: "Anypókritos" (ἀνυπόκριτος, G505) means "without hypocrisy, unfeigned"—a key Pauline qualifier for genuine faith and love (cf. Rom 12:9; 2 Cor 6:6).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: from which some, having missed the mark, turned aside unto vain talk,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: desiring to be teachers of the law, understanding neither what they say nor concerning what things they confidently affirm.
But we know that the law is good, if one uses it lawfully,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: knowing this, that for a righteous one the law is not appointed, but for the lawless and unruly, for the ungodly and sinners, for the unholy and profane, for strikers of fathers and strikers of mothers, for murderers,
verses.note: GREEK: "Dikaiō nómos ou keîtai" (δικαίῳ νόμος οὐ κεῖται)—the law "is not laid down" or "does not lie appointed" for the righteous. "Keîtai" suggests a law set in place as a restraint. The vice list that follows echoes the structure of the Decalogue, moving from offenses against God to offenses against neighbor. THEOLOGICAL: Paul does not abrogate the law but clarifies its function: it convicts the lawless, not those already walking in faith and love (cf. Gal 3:19-25).

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: for the sexually immoral, for men who lie with males, for slave-traders, for liars, for the perjured, and if there is any other thing that opposes the sound teaching,
verses.note: GREEK: "Arsenokoítais" (ἀρσενοκοίταις, G733) is a compound from "arsēn" (male) and "koítē" (bed), likely coined from the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13 (meta arsenos koitēn). "Andrapodistais" (ἀνδραποδισταῖς, G405) denotes slave-traders or kidnappers who enslave free persons—a hapax legomenon in the NT. HISTORICAL: Slave-trading was condemned in Jewish law (Exod 21:16; Deut 24:7) and its inclusion here aligns the vice list with Torah categories.

verses.flow: according to the gospel of the glory of the blessed God, with which I was entrusted.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The gospel of the glory of the blessed God" is a remarkable genitive chain: the gospel is itself the radiance of God's glory, not merely a report about it. "Makaríou" (μακαρίου) applied to God is rare in the NT (cf. 1 Tim 6:15), emphasizing God's transcendent blessedness. Paul's entrustment (episteúthēn, passive of pisteúō) stresses that the gospel is a divine deposit, not a human invention.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I have gratitude toward the one who empowered me, Christ Jesus our Lord, that he counted me faithful, appointing me unto service,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: though formerly being a blasphemer and a persecutor and an insolent man; but I was shown mercy, because acting in ignorance I did it in unbelief.
And the grace of our Lord super-abounded with faith and love that is in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Hyperepleonasen" (ὑπερεπλεόνασεν, G5250) is a hapax legomenon in the NT—an intensified compound meaning "super-abounded" or "overflowed beyond all measure." The prefix hyper- intensifies the already strong pleonázō. TYPOLOGY: This mirrors Paul's principle in Rom 5:20: "where sin increased, grace super-abounded," with Paul's own life as the living demonstration.

verses.flow: Faithful is the word and worthy of all acceptance, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pistós ho lógos" (πιστὸς ὁ λόγος)—"Faithful is the word"—is a distinctive formula appearing five times in the Pastoral Epistles, introducing core confessional statements. This is the first and arguably most central: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. TYPOLOGY: Christ's entrance "into the world" (eis ton kosmon) echoes the incarnational theology of John 1:9-14 and the divine mission from heaven to earth (cf. Phil 2:6-8). Paul's self-designation as "foremost" (prōtos) of sinners is not rhetorical humility but genuine theological conviction grounding the universality of grace.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But on account of this I was shown mercy, that in me first Christ Jesus might display all his longsuffering, for a pattern to those who are about to believe upon him unto life eternal.
Now to the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory unto the ages of the ages. Amen.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This doxology ascribes four attributes to God: King of the ages (basileî tōn aiṓnōn), immortal (aphthártō), invisible (aorátō), only God (mónō theō). DIVINE_COUNCIL: "King of the ages" positions God as sovereign over all temporal and cosmic epochs, ruling over all heavenly and earthly powers. "Mónō theō" (the only God) does not deny the divine identity of Christ but affirms monotheistic ontology within which Christ operates as Lord—consistent with the Shema framework (Deut 6:4) as applied in 1 Cor 8:6. The doxological formula "unto the ages of the ages" (eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn) is the strongest Greek expression of eternity.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural feminine

verses.flow: This charge I entrust to you, child Timothy, according to the prophecies previously made concerning you, that by them you might wage the good warfare,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: holding faith and a good conscience, which some, having thrust away, concerning the faith suffered shipwreck,
of whom are Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom I delivered over to Satan, that they might be disciplined not to blaspheme.
verses.note: GREEK: "Parédōka tō Satanâ" (παρέδωκα τῷ Σατανᾷ)—"I delivered over to Satan"—parallels 1 Cor 5:5 and likely denotes formal excommunication, placing the offender outside the protective boundary of the ekklēsia and into the domain of the adversary. THEOLOGICAL: The purpose is remedial, not destructive: "hína paideuthōsi" (ἵνα παιδευθῶσι)—"that they might be disciplined." Paideúō (G3811) carries the sense of child-training and correction, indicating hope for restoration. HISTORICAL: Hymenaeus reappears in 2 Tim 2:17 still teaching falsely; Alexander may be the coppersmith of 2 Tim 4:14.

verses.flow: I urge, therefore, first of all, that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings be made on behalf of all men,
verses.note: GREEK: Paul uses four distinct terms for prayer: δεήσεις (deēseis, Strong's G1162) — specific petitions arising from need; προσευχάς (proseuchas, G4335) — general prayers directed to God; ἐντεύξεις (enteuxeis, G1783) — intercessions, literally "meetings with" or "petitions to a superior," used only here and in 1 Tim 4:5 in the NT; and εὐχαριστίας (eucharistias, G2169) — thanksgivings. The breadth of terms emphasizes the comprehensive nature of the prayer life commanded.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: on behalf of kings and all those being in authority, that a quiet and peaceable life we may lead in all godliness and dignity.
This is good and acceptable before God our Savior,
who desires all men to be saved and to come to a full knowledge of the truth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase πάντας ἀνθρώπους θέλει σωθῆναι ("desires all men to be saved") uses θέλει (thelei, G2309), expressing God's volitional desire. The word ἐπίγνωσιν (epignōsin, G1922) is not mere γνῶσις (gnōsis, "knowledge") but a full, experiential, precise knowledge of truth. This verse grounds the universal scope of prayer in verses 1-2 in the universal salvific will of God (cf. 2 Pet 3:9; Titus 2:11).

verses.flow: For there is one God, and one mediator of God and men, the man Christ Jesus,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The emphatic εἷς... εἷς ("one... one") echoes the Shema of Deut 6:4 (LXX: εἷς ἐστιν) and applies monotheistic logic to the mediatorial office of Christ. GREEK: μεσίτης (mesitēs, G3316) denotes one who stands between two parties to reconcile them; used of Moses in Gal 3:19-20 but here applied uniquely to Christ as the sole mediator between God and humanity. The apposition ἄνθρωπος Χριστὸς Ἰησοῦς ("the man Christ Jesus") stresses his true humanity as essential to his mediatorial role. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Christ's unique mediatorship transcends all angelic or spiritual intermediaries, establishing his singular authority in the divine economy.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: the one having given himself as a ransom on behalf of all — the testimony in its own appointed times —
verses.note: GREEK: ἀντίλυτρον (antilytron, G487) is a NT hapax legomenon — found only here. It intensifies λύτρον (lytron, "ransom," cf. Matt 20:28; Mark 10:45) with the prefix ἀντί ("in place of"), emphasizing a substitutionary ransom — a price paid in exchange for another. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the Levitical substitutionary sacrificial system and fulfills the Suffering Servant typology of Isa 53:10-12, where the servant gives his life as an offering for many. The phrase τὸ μαρτύριον καιροῖς ἰδίοις ("the testimony in its own appointed times") indicates that Christ's self-giving was revealed according to God's sovereign timetable (cf. Gal 4:4; Eph 1:10).

verses.flow: unto which I was appointed a herald and an apostle — I speak truth, I do not lie — a teacher of the nations in faith and truth.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: I desire, therefore, that the men pray in every place, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and disputing.
Likewise also that women adorn themselves in orderly apparel with modesty and self-control, not with braided hair and gold or pearls or costly garments,

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural feminine

verses.flow: but what is fitting for women professing godliness — through good works.
Let a woman learn in quietness, in all submission.
But to teach I do not permit a woman, nor to exercise authority over a man, but to be in quietness.
verses.note: GREEK: αὐθεντεῖν (authentein, G831) is a NT hapax legomenon, appearing only here. Its precise meaning is debated: it may range from "to exercise authority" to "to domineer" or "to usurp authority." The rarity of the term makes definitive conclusions difficult, but the context pairs it with διδάσκειν (didaskein, "to teach"), suggesting a specific ecclesial function rather than a general social principle. HISTORICAL: In the Ephesian context, the cult of Artemis and various Gnostic-leaning teachings may have given rise to women propagating false doctrine (cf. 1 Tim 1:3-7; 2 Tim 3:6-7), which may inform the specificity of this instruction.

verses.flow: For Adam was formed first, then Eve.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And Adam was not deceived, but the woman, having been thoroughly deceived, has come into transgression.
Yet she shall be saved through the childbearing, if they remain in faith and love and holiness, with self-control.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the most debated verses in the Pauline corpus. "The childbearing" (τῆς τεκνογονίας, tēs teknogonias, G5042) carries the definite article τῆς, suggesting not childbearing in general but a specific childbearing. TYPOLOGY: Many interpreters see an allusion to the protoevangelium of Gen 3:15 — the "seed of the woman" who would crush the serpent's head — thus pointing to the birth of Christ as the means of salvation that reverses Eve's transgression. The shift from singular σωθήσεται ("she shall be saved") to plural μείνωσιν ("they remain") likely broadens the application from Eve (or womankind collectively) to all women who persevere in faith, love, holiness, and self-control.

verses.flow: Faithful is the word: if anyone reaches out for the office of overseer, he desires a good work.
verses.note: GREEK: "Pistos ho logos" (Πιστὸς ὁ λόγος) is a distinctive Pastoral Epistles formula appearing five times (1 Tim 1:15; 3:1; 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11; Titus 3:8). "Episkopēs" (ἐπισκοπῆς, Strong's G1984) means "oversight" or "visitation," from episkopos (overseer). "Oregetai" (ὀρέγεται, G3713) means "stretches out toward" or "aspires to," indicating active, deliberate desire rather than passive wish.

verses.flow: It is necessary, therefore, for the overseer to be above reproach, a husband of one wife, temperate, self-controlled, orderly, hospitable, apt to teach,
verses.note: GREEK: "Episkopon" (ἐπίσκοπον, G1985) means "overseer" — one who watches over. The term was used in the LXX for appointed officials and in Greek civic life for municipal supervisors. "Anepilēmpton" (ἀνεπίλημπτον, G423) is literally "not to be laid hold of," meaning one against whom no charge can be seized upon. THEOLOGICAL: The qualifications center on observable character rather than spiritual gifting, emphasizing that leadership in God's household requires proven moral integrity visible to all.

verses.flow: not given to wine, not a striker, but gentle, not quarrelsome, not a lover of money,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: presiding well over his own household, having children in subjection with all dignity—
(but if anyone does not know how to preside over his own household, how shall he take care of the assembly of God?)—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul draws a direct analogy between household governance and ecclesial governance. The "oikos" (household) of a man typologically mirrors the "ekklēsia theou" (assembly of God). This reflects the broader biblical pattern where faithfulness in smaller stewardship qualifies one for greater stewardship (Luke 16:10). STRUCTURE: The parenthetical rhetorical question employs an a fortiori (lesser-to-greater) argument.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: not a newly planted one, lest having been puffed up he fall into the judgment of the devil.
verses.note: GREEK: "Neophyton" (νεόφυτον, G3504) is literally "newly planted" — a horticultural metaphor unique in the NT (hapax legomenon). "Typhōtheis" (τυφωθείς, G5187) means "beclouded" or "wrapped in smoke," hence "puffed up with conceit." THEOLOGICAL: The "krima tou diabolou" (judgment of the devil) likely means the same judgment that the devil himself received — pride leading to condemnation. This echoes the divine council theme of a heavenly being falling through self-exaltation (Isa 14:12-15; Ezek 28:17).

verses.flow: And it is necessary also for him to have a good testimony from those outside, lest he fall into reproach and the snare of the devil.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Servants likewise must be dignified, not double-tongued, not giving attention to much wine, not greedy for shameful gain,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: holding the mystery of the faith in a clean conscience.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystērion" (μυστήριον, G3466) here denotes not a hidden secret but divine truth now revealed through the gospel — the mystery of the faith. THEOLOGICAL: The deacon must hold the revealed content of the faith with a "kathara syneidēsei" (clean conscience), binding right doctrine inseparably to right living. Orthodoxy without moral integrity disqualifies one from service.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And let these also be tested first; then let them serve, being without accusation.
Women likewise must be dignified, not slanderers, temperate, faithful in all things.
verses.note: GREEK: "Gynaikas" (γυναῖκας) can mean either "women" or "wives." Debate persists whether Paul refers to deacons' wives, women deacons (deaconesses), or a distinct female office. The parallel structure with "hōsautōs" (likewise) mirrors v.8, suggesting a comparable category of service. "Diabolous" (διαβόλους, G1228) here means "slanderers" — the same root as "diabolos" (devil), literally "one who throws across" accusations.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let servants be husbands of one wife, presiding well over children and their own households.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For those who have served well acquire for themselves a good standing and much boldness in the faith that is in Christ Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: These things I write to you, hoping to come to you shortly;

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: but if I delay, that you may know how one ought to conduct oneself in the household of God, which is the assembly of the living God, the pillar and foundation of the truth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "oikos theou" (household of God) is identified as the "ekklēsia theou zōntos" (assembly of the living God). Paul then applies two architectural metaphors: "stylos" (pillar, G4769) and "hedraiōma" (foundation or buttress, G1477 — hapax legomenon in NT). The assembly bears and upholds the truth as a pillar supports a temple. TYPOLOGY: The pillar imagery echoes the temple pillars Jachin and Boaz (1 Kgs 7:21) and the eschatological promise that the faithful will be made a pillar in God's temple (Rev 3:12).

verses.flow: And confessedly great is the mystery of godliness: He who was manifested in the flesh, was justified in the Spirit, was seen by angels, was proclaimed among the nations, was believed on in the world, was taken up in glory.
verses.note: GREEK: The textual variant here is significant: the earliest manuscripts read "hos" (Ὅς, "He who") rather than the later "theos" (God) or "ho" (which). The six-line confessional hymn follows a pattern of paired contrasts (flesh/Spirit, angels/nations, world/glory). THEOLOGICAL: This is among the earliest christological hymns, confessing Christ's incarnation ("manifested in flesh"), vindication ("justified in Spirit"), angelic revelation ("seen by angels"), global proclamation ("proclaimed among nations"), faith response ("believed on in the world"), and heavenly exaltation ("taken up in glory"). DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Was seen by angels" places Christ's work within the divine council framework — the heavenly beings themselves witnessed and were made subject to the revelation of the incarnate Lord (cf. Eph 3:10; 1 Pet 1:12).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But the Spirit expressly says that in latter times some shall depart from the faith, giving heed to deceiving spirits and teachings of demons,
verses.note: GREEK: "πνεύμασι πλάνοις" (pneumasin planois) — "deceiving spirits" or "wandering spirits." The adjective planos (G4108) carries the sense of roaming or leading astray, used also of the "deceiving spirit" in the LXX tradition. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Paul here acknowledges a spiritual realm of hostile entities — demons actively producing doctrinal systems ("teachings of demons") channeled through human agents. This reflects the Second Temple understanding of rebellious divine-council members who lead nations and individuals into apostasy (cf. Deut 32:17; Ps 106:37; 1 Cor 10:20).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: through the hypocrisy of speakers-of-lies, having been seared in their own conscience,
verses.note: GREEK: "κεκαυστηριασμένων" (kekaustēriasmenōn, G2743) is a perfect passive participle meaning "having been branded" or "seared as with a hot iron." This is a NT hapax legomenon. The perfect tense indicates a completed action with lasting effect — their conscience has been permanently cauterized, rendering them insensible to moral conviction. The imagery may evoke the Roman practice of branding slaves or criminals.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb perfect active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: forbidding to marry, commanding to abstain from foods which God created for reception with thanksgiving by those who are faithful and have fully known the truth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The false teachers impose ascetic prohibitions — forbidding marriage and commanding abstinence from foods — that contradict the creational goodness affirmed in Genesis 1. Paul's phrase "which God created for reception with thanksgiving" (εἰς μετάλημψιν μετὰ εὐχαριστίας) grounds the argument in creation theology: God's good gifts are not to be refused but received with gratitude. HISTORICAL: Such proto-gnostic or encratite tendencies were present in the Ephesian milieu, reflecting dualistic views that denigrated the material world.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: For every creature of God is good, and nothing is to be rejected when received with thanksgiving,
for it is sanctified through the word of God and intercession.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐντεύξεως" (enteuxeōs, G1783) — "intercession" or "prayer." This term appears only here and in 1 Tim 2:1 in the NT. The sanctification of created things occurs through two means: the word of God (logos theou) and prayer. THEOLOGICAL: This establishes a sacramental principle — the material creation is consecrated not by its own nature alone but through God's spoken word and the prayer of the faithful (cf. Gen 1:31).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Laying these things before the brothers, you shall be a good servant of Christ Jesus, being nourished in the words of the faith and of the good teaching which you have closely followed.
But the profane and old-womanish myths refuse; and train yourself rather toward godliness.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For bodily training is profitable for a little, but godliness is profitable for all things, holding promise of the life that now is and of the one to come.
Faithful is the word, and worthy of all acceptance.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: "Faithful is the word" (πιστὸς ὁ λόγος) is one of the five "faithful sayings" unique to the Pastoral Epistles (1 Tim 1:15; 3:1; 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11; Titus 3:8). Scholars debate whether this formula points backward to v.8 or forward to v.10; the context favors a reference to v.8's promise regarding godliness, though the connection to the hope expressed in v.10 is also strong.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For unto this we labor and strive, because we have set our hope upon the living God, who is Savior of all men, especially of those who believe.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Savior of all men, especially of those who believe" (σωτὴρ πάντων ἀνθρώπων μάλιστα πιστῶν) — "especially" (μάλιστα, G3122) does not negate the universal scope but intensifies the particular application. God's saving provision extends universally in its offer and preserving goodness, but is realized eschatologically and salvifically "especially" in those who believe. GREEK: "ζῶντι" (zōnti) — "living" — stands in deliberate contrast to the dead idols and deceiving spirits of vv.1-2; the living God alone is Savior.

verses.flow: These things command and teach.
Let no one despise your youth, but become a pattern of the faithful in word, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity.
verses.note: GREEK: "τύπος" (typos, G5179) — literally "a strike-mark," hence "pattern, model, example." Timothy is to be a visible imprint of Christlike character for the believing community. HISTORICAL: Timothy's youth (νεότης, neotēs) would have been culturally significant in Greco-Roman society, where authority was typically vested in older men. Paul counters potential dismissal not by asserting Timothy's rights but by calling him to exemplary living across five domains: word, conduct, love, faith, and purity.

verses.flow: Until I come, give heed to the reading, to the exhortation, to the teaching.
Do not neglect the gift that is in you, which was given to you through prophecy with the laying on of the hands of the council of elders.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "charisma" (χάρισμα, G5486) given to Timothy came through a dual instrumentality: prophetic utterance and the corporate laying on of hands by the presbyterion (πρεσβυτέριον, G4244), the council of elders. This reflects early church ordination practice where the Spirit's gifting is mediated through the prophetic community and confirmed by eldership authority (cf. Acts 13:1-3; 2 Tim 1:6). GREEK: πρεσβυτέριον denotes not individual elders but the collective body or council of elders, a term borrowed from Jewish synagogue governance.

verses.flow: These things practice; in these things be, so that your progress may be manifest to all.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Give heed to yourself and to the teaching; continue in them, for doing this you shall save both yourself and those who hear you.
Do not rebuke an elder, but exhort him as a father; younger men as brothers;
elder women as mothers; younger women as sisters, in all purity.
Honor widows who are truly widows.
verses.note: GREEK: "Honor" (τίμα, timáō, Strong's G5091) carries connotations of both respect and material support, as the same root appears in v17 for "double honor" applied to elders. "Truly widows" (ὄντως χήρας) distinguishes those genuinely destitute from those with family resources—a technical category for the early assembly's care system.

verses.flow: But if any widow has children or grandchildren, let them learn first to show piety toward their own household and to render recompense to their forebears, for this is acceptable before God.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Now she who is truly a widow and has been left alone has set her hope upon God and continues in supplications and prayers night and day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The portrait of the "true widow" echoes the prophetess Anna (Luke 2:36-37), who likewise devoted herself to prayer and fasting in the temple night and day. The perfect tense "has set her hope" (ἤλπικεν) indicates a settled, abiding state of trust in God, not a momentary disposition.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But she who lives in self-indulgence, though living, has died.
And these things command, that they may be above reproach.
But if anyone does not provide for his own, and especially for those of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul employs the strongest possible language: denying the faith (τὴν πίστιν ἤρνηται) and being worse than an unbeliever (ἀπίστου χείρων). This grounds household provision not merely in social convention but in the very nature of covenant faithfulness. Even pagans in the Greco-Roman world recognized familial obligation.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Let a widow be enrolled who is not less than sixty years of age, having been the wife of one husband,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine
verb present passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: being attested by good works: if she brought up children, if she showed hospitality to strangers, if she washed the feet of the saints, if she relieved the afflicted, if she diligently followed every good work.
verses.note: GREEK: "Brought up children" (ἐτεκνοτρόφησεν, teknotrophéō, G5044) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. "Showed hospitality to strangers" (ἐξενοδόχησεν, xenodochéō, G3580) is likewise a NT hapax. The washing of saints' feet recalls Jesus' act of humble service (John 13:5-14), suggesting that the enrolled widow embodies Christlike servanthood.

verses.flow: But younger widows refuse, for when they grow wanton against Christ, they desire to marry,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: having condemnation, because they have cast aside their first faith.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural feminine
verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And at the same time also they learn to be idle, going about from house to house; and not only idle, but also gossips and busybodies, speaking the things which are not fitting.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: I will therefore that younger widows marry, bear children, manage the household, giving no occasion to the adversary for reproach.
verses.note: GREEK: "The adversary" (τῷ ἀντικειμένῳ) is a present middle participle meaning "the one who opposes," which could refer to Satan or to human opponents of the faith. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's counsel is pastoral and strategic—minimizing occasion for slander against the assembly. "Guide the household" (οἰκοδεσποτεῖν, G3616) is another NT hapax, granting the wife significant domestic authority.

verses.flow: For already some have turned aside after Satan.
If any believing woman has widows, let her relieve them, and let the assembly not be burdened, that it may relieve those who are truly widows.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially those laboring in the word and in teaching.
verses.note: GREEK: "Rule well" (καλῶς προεστῶτες) uses proïstēmi (G4291), meaning to stand before, lead, or manage. "Double honor" (διπλῆς τιμῆς) almost certainly includes financial remuneration, as v18 immediately supports this with Scripture. The distinction "especially those laboring in word and teaching" implies not all elders held a primary teaching function.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the Scripture says, "You shall not muzzle an ox while it treads out the grain," and, "The laborer is worthy of his wages."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul cites Deuteronomy 25:4 (LXX) alongside a saying attributed to the Lord Jesus (Luke 10:7), placing both on equal footing as "Scripture" (ἡ γραφή). This is a significant witness to the early recognition of apostolic and dominical words as carrying Scriptural authority alongside the Torah.

verses.flow: Against an elder do not receive an accusation, except upon two or three witnesses.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Those who are sinning, rebuke before all, that the rest also may have fear.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus and the elect angels, that you keep these things without prejudgment, doing nothing according to partiality.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The solemn charge is made "before God and Christ Jesus and the elect angels" (τῶν ἐκλεκτῶν ἀγγέλων). This tripartite witness formula invokes the divine council as the heavenly tribunal before which Timothy's ministry will be judged. "Elect angels" distinguishes faithful heavenly beings from fallen ones, reflecting the cosmic dimension of ecclesial governance. GREEK: "Prejudgment" (πρόκριμα, prókrima, G4299) is a NT hapax, meaning a verdict rendered before hearing the case.

verses.flow: Lay hands upon no one hastily, neither share in the sins of others. Keep yourself pure.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The laying on of hands (χεῖρας ἐπιτίθει) likely refers to ordination of elders, connecting back to the instructions about elder discipline in vv17-20. Hasty ordination implicates one in the sins that an unvetted leader may commit. "Keep yourself pure" (σεαυτὸν ἁγνὸν τήρει) forms a bookend with the purity theme introduced in v2.

verses.flow: No longer drink only water, but use a little wine on account of your stomach and your frequent ailments.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: The sins of some men are evident beforehand, going before them to judgment; but for some they also follow after.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This proverbial observation provides the theological rationale for patient discernment in vv21-22. Some sins are immediately manifest; others surface only later. Therefore both hasty ordination and hasty accusation are dangerous. The eschatological dimension of "judgment" (κρίσιν) reminds Timothy that God's final assessment will reveal all hidden things (Rom 2:16; 1 Cor 4:5).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: Likewise also the good works are evident beforehand, and those which are otherwise are not able to be hidden.
As many as are under a yoke as bondservants, let them regard their own masters as worthy of all honor, so that the name of God and the teaching may not be blasphemed.
verses.note: GREEK: "Under a yoke" (ὑπὸ ζυγόν, hypo zygon) is a metaphor for the condition of servitude. "Bondservants" (δοῦλοι, douloi, Strong's G1401) denotes slaves in the Roman household system. "Masters" (δεσπότας, despotas, Strong's G1203) differs from κύριος and specifically denotes absolute ownership. HISTORICAL: Paul addresses the social reality of slavery within the Roman Empire, where an estimated one-third of the population were enslaved. The concern is that dishonoring pagan masters would bring reproach upon the gospel.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And those having believing masters, let them not despise them because they are brothers, but rather let them serve, because those receiving the benefit are faithful and beloved. These things teach and exhort.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: If anyone teaches a different doctrine and does not come to the sound words, those of our Lord Jesus Christ, and to the teaching that is according to godliness,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: he has been puffed up with conceit, understanding nothing, but being sick concerning disputes and word-battles, from which come envy, strife, blasphemies, evil suspicions,
verses.note: GREEK: "Puffed up with conceit" (τετύφωται, tetyphōtai, Strong's G5187) literally means "wrapped in smoke" or "beclouded"—a vivid metaphor for intellectual delusion. "Word-battles" (λογομαχίας, logomachias, Strong's G3055) is a compound of λόγος + μάχη and is a NT hapax legomenon, describing contentious verbal disputes that produce no edification.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: constant frictions of men corrupted in mind and deprived of the truth, supposing godliness to be a means of gain.
But godliness with contentment is great gain.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse stands as a pivotal counter-thesis to the false teachers' equation of godliness with material profit (v.5). "Contentment" (αὐταρκείας, autarkeias, Strong's G841) was a key Stoic virtue meaning self-sufficiency, but Paul redefines it as sufficiency found in God rather than in oneself (cf. Phil 4:11-12). True gain (πορισμός, porismos) is godliness accompanied by this God-centered satisfaction.

verses.flow: For we brought nothing into the world, because neither are we able to carry anything out.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But having food and covering, with these we shall be content.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But those desiring to be rich fall into temptation and a snare and many foolish and harmful desires, which plunge men into ruin and destruction.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For a root of all the evils is the love of money, which some reaching after have been led astray from the faith and have pierced themselves through with many sorrows.
verses.note: GREEK: "Love of money" (φιλαργυρία, philargyria, Strong's G5365) is a compound of φίλος (love) + ἀργύριον (silver). Note Paul writes "a root" (ῥίζα, rhiza, without the article in some manuscripts), not necessarily "the root"—money-love is one fundamental root among evils, not the sole origin of all evil. "Pierced themselves through" (περιέπειραν, periepeiran, Strong's G4044) is another NT hapax legomenon, depicting a violent self-inflicted impaling on sorrows.

verses.flow: But you, O man of God, flee these things; and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, endurance, gentleness of spirit.
Fight the good fight of the faith; lay hold of the eternal life, into which you were called and confessed the good confession before many witnesses.
verses.note: GREEK: "Fight" (ἀγωνίζου, agōnizou) and "fight" (ἀγῶνα, agōna) form a cognate accusative construction—literally "agonize the good agony." The athletic imagery evokes the Greek games. THEOLOGICAL: "Lay hold" (ἐπιλαβοῦ, epilabou) is an aorist imperative suggesting decisive, urgent action. The eternal life is both a present possession and a future hope to be grasped. Timothy's "good confession" likely refers to his baptismal confession or ordination vow (cf. v.13; 2 Tim 1:6).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: I charge you before God, the one giving life to all things, and Christ Jesus, the one having witnessed the good confession before Pontius Pilate,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The solemn charge invokes both God the Father as life-giver (ζῳογονοῦντος, zōogonountos—the one who gives life to all things, echoing creation and resurrection power) and Christ Jesus as the faithful witness. HISTORICAL: The reference to Pontius Pilate anchors the faith in concrete history. Christ's "good confession" before Pilate (John 18:36-37) serves as the paradigm for Timothy's own faithfulness under pressure. TYPOLOGY: As Christ confessed before the earthly ruler, so Timothy must confess before his generation—the pattern of faithful witness unto potential suffering.

verses.flow: that you keep the commandment spotless and beyond reproach until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: which in his own times shall show forth the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of those reigning and Lord of those exercising lordship,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Blessed and only Sovereign" (μακάριος καὶ μόνος δυνάστης) is a doxological title applied to God who orchestrates the appearing of Christ. "King of those reigning and Lord of those exercising lordship" (βασιλεὺς τῶν βασιλευόντων καὶ κύριος τῶν κυριευόντων) directly challenges Roman imperial claims to supreme authority (cf. Rev 17:14; 19:16). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This title asserts God's absolute sovereignty over all rulers, both human and spiritual—all powers, thrones, and dominions are subordinate to the one true Sovereign. The "own times" (καιροῖς ἰδίοις) emphasizes divine prerogative over eschatological timing.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: the only one having immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no one of men has seen nor is able to see; to whom be honor and eternal dominion. Amen.
verses.note: GREEK: "Unapproachable" (ἀπρόσιτον, aprositon, Strong's G676) is a NT hapax legomenon—light so radiant that no mortal can draw near. THEOLOGICAL: "The only one having immortality" (ὁ μόνος ἔχων ἀθανασίαν) does not deny that believers receive immortality, but affirms that God alone possesses it intrinsically and inherently. This echoes the theophany traditions of the Old Testament where God's glory is unbearable to human sight (Exod 33:20; 1 Kgs 8:11). The language forms a barrier between Creator and creature that only divine revelation can bridge.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: To the rich in the present age, charge them not to be high-minded, nor to have set their hope upon the uncertainty of riches, but upon God, the one richly providing us all things for enjoyment,
to do good, to be rich in good works, to be generous in giving, ready to share,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: treasuring up for themselves a good foundation for the coming age, so that they may lay hold of that which is truly life.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Treasuring up a good foundation" (ἀποθησαυρίζοντας θεμέλιον καλόν) mixes metaphors of treasure-storing and foundation-laying, echoing Jesus' teaching on storing treasures in heaven (Matt 6:19-20) and building on a firm foundation (Matt 7:24-25). GREEK: "That which is truly life" (τῆς ὄντως ζωῆς) uses the adverb ὄντως (ontōs, "really, truly") to distinguish authentic eternal life from mere biological or material existence.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: O Timothy, guard the deposit entrusted to you, turning away from the profane empty utterances and oppositions of the falsely-named knowledge,
verses.note: GREEK: "The deposit" (παραθήκην, parathēkēn, Strong's G3866) is a legal-banking term for something entrusted to another for safekeeping—here the apostolic teaching and sound doctrine entrusted to Timothy. "Falsely-named knowledge" (ψευδωνύμου γνώσεως) is a direct polemic; ψευδώνυμος (pseudōnymos, Strong's G5581) means "bearing a false name." HISTORICAL: This likely targets early forms of proto-Gnostic speculation or syncretistic Jewish-Hellenistic speculative systems circulating in Ephesus, which claimed superior "knowledge" (gnōsis) while contradicting apostolic teaching.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: which some professing have missed the mark concerning the faith. Grace be with you.
